content.full_text:
ROBERT D. HANSEN
Attorney At Law

FPPC

661 Pamlar Avenue
San Jose, California 95128
(408) 287-1500

FEB 1 7 1992

February 7, 1992


Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 "J" Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Hallabrin,

I serve as legal counsel to an unsuccessful candidate for mayor of the City of San Jose. During the past weeks I have had several conversations with Ms. Linda Morrow of the Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") staff about the committee's efforts to retire various accrued expenses from the campaign. She was gracious enough to forward me a copy of a June 28, 1977 letter to Mr. Robert C. Summers from the Commission regarding the reporting requirements of vendors that forgive debts owed to them by political campaigns. The letter and a recent court decision raises several unresolved matters about our committee's efforts and I am writing to you for the Commission's opinions that might hopefully clarify the matters.

The candidate formed a controlled political committee for the 1990 June primary and November general elections for the office of mayor of the City of San Jose. The committee was involved only in the local municipal race and had no involvement in any state legislative, constitutional office, or initiative campaigns. Many hundreds of thousands of dollars were raised during the course of the campaign and the money was used to pay for all of the expenses of the committee except for approximately $80,000 in accrued expenses from the primary election that is still owed to the candidate, a campaign consultant, and to a local company for the rental of office space. The City of San Jose is a charter city under California law and has enacted a municipal ordinance limiting contributions to a mayoral candidate to no more than $500.00 in any fiscal year from any one individual. All of the appropriate campaign reports have been filed in a timely manner with the San Jose City Clerk's office and the committee has fully complied with the San Jose Code and California law.

The committee still exists today but it is for the most part inactive. For a variety of reasons, including the candidate's desire to seek a city council seat during the 1992 elections and the formation of a second and separate controlled committee for those campaigns, the mayoral committee may not be able to retire the 1990 primary campaign debt. The mayoral committee, however, appears to have four options for reducing the debt: first, raising additional funds and paying the debts over an extended period of time; second, the creditors contributing portions of the accrued expenses as non-monetary contributions to the campaign while not exceeding local contribution limits; third, complete or partial default by the committee upon the obligations to pay the debts; or fourth, negotiating a reduced value of the debts based on external factors and commercial market conditions. It is problematic if any one approach or a combination of the options will be successful in retiring all or any portion of the debts.

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
February 7, 1992
Page 2

To aid the committee in its evaluation of these options, we are requesting the Commission's
advice on the following questions:
1)      The Commission's June 28, 1977 letter to Mr. Summers refers to "bona fide business
judgement that all or part of the debt is uncollectable." Can you provide guidance as to what factors or
tests are considered by the Commission in determining what constitutes a "bona fide business
judgement?"

2)      The Commission's June 28, 1977 letter provides guidance to the creditors in
determining the creditor's filing obligations upon forgiveness or reduction of a debt. Is the letter also
applicable to a debtor committee in determining its obligations and options?

On a related matter, I have been following the progress of Service Employees International
Union, AFL-CIO, et al. v. Fair Political Practices Commission, Case No. CIVS 89-0433 LKK-JFM. The City
of San Jose's limits on campaign contributions makes it very difficult for the mayoral committee to reduce its
debt if any portion of the forgiven or uncollectable debt exceeds the fiscal year limit on contributions. In
light of today's decision by the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals in the case, can the Commission provide any
comments as to the constitutionality of contribution limits for campaigns governed by California law in
general, as well as factors affecting the constitutionality of the City of San Jose's campaign contribution
limits?

I very much appreciate your attention to my request. Should you have any questions about this
letter or desire additional facts in developing the Commission's response, please contact me at telephone
(408) 287-1500. Written information can also be transmitted to me at facsimile line (408) 287-1536. Again,
thank you for your assistance.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Robert D. Hansen
Attorney At Law


RDH:ls

sections.question: 1) The Commission's June 28, 1977 letter to Mr. Summers refers to "bona fide business judgement that all or part of the debt is uncollectable." Can you provide guidance as to what factors or tests are considered by the Commission in determining what constitutes a "bona fide business judgement?" 2) The Commission's June 28, 1977 letter provides guidance to the creditors in determining the creditor's filing obligations upon forgiveness or reduction of a debt. Is the letter also applicable to a debtor committee in determining its obligations and options? On a related matter, I have been following the progress of Service Employees International Union, AFL-CIO, et al. v. Fair Political Practices Commission, Case No. CIVS 89-0433 LKK-JFM. The City of San Jose's limits on campaign contributions makes it very difficult for the mayoral committee to reduce its debts if any portion of the forgiven or uncollectable debt exceeds the fiscal year limit on contributions. In light of today's decision by the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals in the case, can the Commission provide any comments as to the constitutionality of contribution limits for campaigns governed by California law in general, as well as factors affecting the constitutionality of the City of San Jose's campaign contribution limits?
sections.question_synthetic: The requester seeks FPPC guidance on how to evaluate debt forgiveness under the bona fide business judgement standard, whether prior FPPC guidance applies to debtor committees, and how a recent Ninth Circuit decision affects the constitutionality of San Jose's campaign contribution limits in the context of retiring campaign debts.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from outside counsel, not an FPPC response. No formal CONCLUSION or SHORT ANSWER section present. Document contains three related questions about campaign debt retirement, vendor debt forgiveness standards, and contribution limit constitutionality. No withdrawal, declination, or other closure indicated.

embedding.summary: An attorney representing an unsuccessful 1990 San Jose mayoral campaign requests FPPC advice on options for retiring approximately $80,000 in accrued campaign debts, specifically regarding bona fide business judgement standards for debt forgiveness, applicability of prior FPPC guidance to debtor committees, and constitutional implications of local contribution limits in light of a recent Ninth Circuit decision.
